summary:
Roger Bacon (c. 1214/1220–1292 AD) was an English Franciscan friar, philosopher, and early advocate of empirical inquiry whose writings prefigured the scientific method and whose encyclopedic ambitions to reform medieval education through mathematics, experimental science, and direct observation made him an anomalous and often misunderstood figure in 13th-century intellectual life. Educated at Oxford and Paris — the two leading universities of his day — Bacon came under the influence of Robert Grosseteste, the Oxford polymath who championed the role of mathematics in natural philosophy, and developed a vision of science as the systematic study of nature through experiment, controlled observation, and mathematical description rather than purely through textual authority.

Bacon's major works — the Opus Majus, Opus Minus, and Opus Tertium, written at the command of Pope Clement IV in the 1260s — were encyclopedic surveys of knowledge arguing for curriculum reform: that theology required sound knowledge of languages (Greek, Hebrew, Arabic), mathematics, optics, and experimental science. His work on optics was particularly significant, drawing on the Arabic tradition of Ibn al-Haytham to analyze lenses, mirrors, and the anatomy of vision, and he made early observations about spectacles and the potential for telescopes and other optical instruments. He discussed gunpowder's composition (in deliberately obscure anagram form) before its widespread use in Europe, and speculated about flying machines and self-propelled ships. Bacon was later imprisoned by his Franciscan order, possibly for suspected magical practices or unauthorized teachings, though the details are obscure. His actual experimental contributions were limited, but his insistence on mathematics and observation as the foundations of natural knowledge gave him legendary status as a prophet of the [[event:2239|Scientific Revolution]] centuries before it arrived.
